sources.evidence: WebFetch returned a live GitHub repo page titled "SAW — SAFe Agentic Workflow AI Agent Harness for Multi-Agent Team Workflows" (not a 404). README confirms every claimed element: a .claude folder with 24 slash commands and 18 model-invoked skills, hooks (hooks-config.json), 11 SAFe agent profiles, and explicit handoff prompt templates between roles (e.g. "QAS validation complete for [TICKET]... Approved for RTE."). MIT licensed, author J. Scott Graham. Minor naming nuance: SAW expands to "SAFe (Scaled Agile Framework) Agentic Workflow", not "Safe Agentic Workflow", but the acronym, repo slug, and all claimed contents match.
sources.summary: The repo is a production-oriented AI agent harness template that adapts the Scaled Agile Framework (SAFe) for multi-agent AI teams, targeting Claude Code, Gemini CLI, Codex CLI, and Cursor. It ships a .claude folder implementing a three-layer architecture: Layer 1 hooks (automatic guardrails/blockers via hooks-config.json), Layer 2 user-invoked slash commands (24, e.g. /start-work, /pre-pr), and Layer 3 model-invoked skills (18, with Skills 2.0 frontmatter trigger patterns). It defines 11 SAFe agent role profiles (Backend/Frontend Developer, QAS, System Architect, RTE, ARCHitect-CLI, HITL, etc.) each with role-specific constraints, defined exit states, and canned handoff prompt templates that pass work between roles. Quality control is enforced via a mandatory "stop-the-line" gate (AC/DoD verification before implementation), a blocking QAS gate with iteration authority to bounce work back until all criteria pass, and a three-stage PR review (pattern, architecture, human merge). Configuration spans .harness-manifest.yml, hooks-config.json, settings.json (Gemini), team-config.json, and .env.template, with a role-collapsing policy specifying which roles can be merged (RTE) and which must stay independent (QAS, Security Engineer). MIT licensed by J. Scott Graham (bybren-llc).
patterns: Three-layer harness architecture: hooks (automatic guardrails/blockers) -> commands (user-invoked workflows) -> skills (model-invoked expertise loaded by trigger patterns)
Role-based agent profiles (11 SAFe roles) each with explicit constraints, a defined exit state (e.g. 'Ready for QAS', 'Approved for RTE', 'MERGED'), and preconditions gating entry to the next role

Templated handoff prompts between agents, e.g. 'QAS validation complete for [TICKET]. All criteria PASSED. Evidence posted to Linear. Approved for RTE.' - handoffs are structured strings asserting evidence, not freeform summaries
Mandatory stop-the-line gate: acceptance criteria / definition-of-done must exist and be verified before any implementation starts; non-negotiable blocking check
QAS iteration loop with bounce-back authority: the QA agent can return work to implementers or route to specialists (BSA, Tech Writer, Architect) and loops until all criteria pass
Three-stage PR review pipeline: pattern compliance review -> architecture review -> human (HITL) merge, with humans as the final gate

Role-collapsing policy: explicitly declares which roles may be merged into one agent (RTE) and which must remain independent for integrity (QAS, Security Engineer) - independence as a configurable but enforced property

Config-as-files layout: .harness-manifest.yml for multi-domain sync metadata, hooks-config.json for hook wiring, team-config.json for agent-team settings, .env.template for secrets, markdown+frontmatter for skills/commands/agents
Multi-provider portability: same harness targets Claude Code, Gemini CLI, Codex CLI, and Cursor, with migration tooling (gemini hooks migrate --from-claude) mapping hook configs across vendors
Evidence-based delivery: each gate requires posted evidence (CI green, validation output, Linear links) attached to the handoff before state can advance

sources.summary: The page is a real, live tutorial on harness-guide.com titled "Your First Harness". It teaches the minimal agent harness pattern: a ~50-line Python implementation using an OpenAI-compatible API with a core loop of "call the model, execute tool calls, feed results back, repeat". It breaks the harness into four components (system prompt, JSON-schema tool definitions, tool execution, orchestration loop), maintains state as a role-tagged message list, and implements guardrails including a MAX_TURNS=15 iteration cap and error-as-string handling. It covers extending with additional tools, swapping models, common pitfalls (dropped assistant messages, non-string tool returns, missing iteration limits), and a "What's Missing" section pointing to memory, context windowing, retry logic, security/sandboxing, and skill loading as next steps. Human review/approval is not an explicit section, making the citation's parenthetical slightly imprecise but the core claim (Harness Guide tutorial on the minimal harness pattern) accurate.
patterns: Core agent loop: call model with messages + tool definitions -> if response has tool_calls, execute each, append results, repeat; if plain text, return it

State as an append-only messages list: objects with role (system/user/assistant/tool) and content; assistant messages carry tool_calls (id, function.name, function.arguments); tool messages carry tool_call_id linking result to call
Tool definition format: JSON schema with type=function, function.name, function.description, function.parameters (properties + required)
Tool results must be serialized to strings (json.dumps for dicts) and appended as {role: tool, tool_call_id, content}
Guardrail: hard iteration cap (MAX_TURNS = 15) to prevent infinite/token-burning loops
Guardrail: wrap tool execution in try/except and return error text as the tool result string instead of raising, so the model can self-correct
Ordering invariant: the assistant message containing tool_calls must be appended to history before the tool result messages, or context is lost
Production gaps named as the next layer: memory systems, context windowing, error recovery with retries, security/sandboxing, on-demand skill loading
Further reading anchors: OpenAI Function Calling guide, Anthropic Tool Use guide, ReAct paper (arxiv.org/abs/2210.03629)

sources.evidence: WebFetch returned a live GitHub repo page titled "GitHub - nexu-io/harness-engineering-guide" (512 stars, 61 forks, MIT license, maintained by Nexu, mirrored at harness-guide.com). sources.summary: An MIT-licensed open-source guide (by Nexu, an open-source managed agent platform) defining a harness as "the runtime wrapper that turns a bare language model into an agent." It is structured as an index of chapters spanning beginner material (a 50-line Python first harness, harness-vs-framework decision criteria), core concepts (the think→act→observe agentic loop with budgets and loop detection; tool registry patterns with static/dynamic loading and MCP; two-tier memory with AGENTS.md/MEMORY.md; guardrails covering permissions, sandboxing, and prompt-injection defense), and advanced practice (context engineering, sub-agents, multi-agent orchestration, long-running harness design, classifier-based permissions, eval awareness). It closes with side-by-side implementation analyses of Claude Code, Codex, Cline, Aider, and Cursor, plus real-world case studies. Code examples are in Python and TypeScript; the guide is also published at harness-guide.com with Chinese language support.
patterns: Agentic loop as think-act-observe cycle with explicit iteration/token budgets, parallel tool calls, and loop detection
Tool registry pattern with static and dynamic tool loading, MCP protocol integration, and emphasis on tool description quality
Two-tier memory architecture: daily session logs plus long-term storage, surfaced via AGENTS.md and MEMORY.md convention files
Guardrails: permission models, trust boundaries, sandboxing (Docker/Firecracker, network isolation, filesystem restrictions), prompt injection defense
Context engineering via priority-based context assembly, three-layer compression defense, and token budgeting
Thin harness + thick skills architecture: skill packaging with on-demand loading and a SKILL.md format
Sub-agent Leader-Worker pattern with file-based communication and session isolation
Error handling: error classification, retry strategies, graceful degradation, checkpoint/resume
Multi-agent orchestration patterns: pipeline, fan-out, and supervisor, each with context isolation
Scheduling/automation: cron, heartbeats, event triggers, session targeting
Long-running harness design: context anxiety mitigation, self-evaluation bias, GAN-inspired generator-evaluator split
Managed agents architecture decoupling brain/hands/session with credential isolation
Classifier-based permissions: model-based classifiers replacing manual approval workflows
Eval awareness: detecting test-time behavior and defending against multi-agent amplification
Initializer + coding agent two-phase pattern using a feature-list JSON and startup rituals
Agent teams coordinating parallel execution via git and bisection strategies

sources.evidence: WebFetch returned the full article (no 404, no login wall). Page title: "Building an AI Agent Harness from Scratch: The Architecture Between LLM and Agent", author "The Daily Agent" on DEV Community, published Apr 30. Headings include "What Is an Agent Harness, Really?", "The Minimal Agent Harness", "Problem 1: The Tool Registry Lies", "Problem 3: The Loop Runs Forever", "The Complete Architecture", "Actionable Takeaways". Code examples are in Python and cover a model interface, tool registry, and agent loop — exactly the claimed content.
sources.summary: The article defines an agent harness as the execution environment between the user and the LLM, responsible for the conversation loop, tool dispatch, memory, guardrails, and failure handling. It builds a minimal Python harness (model interface wrapper + tool registry + iterative agent loop), then walks through five failure modes of the naive version: unvalidated tool calls, context bloat, unbounded loops, swallowed errors, and statelessness. Each problem gets a concrete fix: JSON Schema validation before dispatch, a hot-cache context compressor, a budget enforcer (tokens, tool calls, wall time, per-tool caps), structured error classification, and SQLite-backed session/invocation logging. The thesis is that harness quality dominates model choice ("a well-harnessed GPT-3.5 outperforms an unharnessed GPT-4o"), and it closes with actionable takeaways like "start with the loop, not the model" and "enforce budgets in code, not prompts".
patterns: Agent loop: init messages (system + user) -> call model with tool definitions -> if no tool calls, return content; else append response, execute each tool call, append results, repeat; terminate on no-tool-calls or max_iterations
Tool registry data structures: Tool = {name, description, parameters (JSON Schema), fn (Callable)}; ToolRegistry = {tools dict, call_counts dict}
Pre-dispatch validation: check tool exists, all required params present, reject unexpected params, type-check against schema (claimed to catch 60-70% of errors)

Tool execution error handling: catch exceptions and return structured error messages to the model, never bare tracebacks; classify errors as transient, permanent, or unavailable with a suggested action

Budget enforcer with four limits checked before every iteration: max_tokens (e.g. 30,000), max_tool_calls (e.g. 25), max_time_seconds (e.g. 300), max_per_tool_calls (e.g. 5 per tool); enforced in harness code, not in the prompt

Hot-cache memory pattern: always preserve system prompt, keep N recent messages verbatim, summarize older messages (extract lines containing tool:/result:/error:); trigger compression when tiktoken-counted total exceeds max_context_tokens

State persistence in SQLite: sessions table (session_id, created_at, last_active, user_id) and tool_invocations table (session_id, turn_number, tool_name, arguments, result, success, duration_ms) enabling success-rate analytics

Guardrail principles: start with the loop not the model; enforce budgets in code not prompts; compress context aggressively; log every tool invocation with args/result/duration/success; build the harness first, optimize the model second

sources.evidence: WebFetch returned the full article. Page title: "OVRFLO — How To Build Your Own AI Harness". Headings include: "What an AI agent harness actually does", "Prompt-first vs harness-first", "The operating loop", "The five parts of the harness", "Build the minimum viable AI agent harness", "How to grow the harness", "Common mistakes when building AI agent workflows". The article explicitly names Claude, Cursor, and Codex as example agents, and covers scoped delivery, verification/evidence bundles ("proof"), and persistent project memory — matching the claim.
sources.summary: The article argues that reliable AI-agent software delivery comes from a "harness" (a system around the work) rather than ever-longer prompts: "a prompt can start the work. A harness makes the work reliable." It defines a five-part harness — Instructions (mapped onboarding guidance, not one giant prompt), State (persistent project memory that survives session closure), Scope (written boundaries preventing silent expansion), Verification (layered proof from acceptance scenarios through runtime validation to an evidence bundle), and Lifecycle (Draft → Ready → In Progress → Verified → Closed). It prescribes a concrete operating loop (request → requirements → hardening review → acceptance scenarios → work record → implementation → verification → runtime validation → evidence bundle → memory update) and a minimum viable file layout (AGENTS.md plus docs/work-records, docs/acceptance, docs/evidence, docs/decisions with per-task-slug markdown files and check scripts). It closes with common failure modes such as storing memory in chat history, writing acceptance tests after implementation, and declaring work done without evidence. Claude, Cursor, and Codex are named explicitly as the agents the harness applies to.
patterns: Operating loop: request -> requirements -> hardening panel review -> acceptance scenarios -> work record -> implementation -> verification -> runtime validation -> evidence bundle -> project memory update (feeds next request)
Five harness parts: Instructions (mapped onboarding files), State (persistent project memory), Scope (written boundaries), Verification (layered proof), Lifecycle (compounding work states)

Minimum viable harness file structure: AGENTS.md at repo root plus docs/work-records/, docs/acceptance/, docs/evidence/, docs/decisions/ each with _template.md, and scripts/check-work-record + scripts/check-evidence deterministic checks
Naming convention: docs/work-records/<task-slug>.md, docs/acceptance/<task-slug>.md, docs/evidence/<task-slug>.md, docs/decisions/YYYY-MM-DD-<decision-slug>
Verification is layered: acceptance scenarios -> deterministic checks -> specialist review -> runtime validation -> evidence bundle (tests passing alone is not proof)
Acceptance scenarios are written before implementation, never derived from the finished code
Work lifecycle state machine: Draft -> Ready -> In Progress -> Verified -> Closed

Guardrails (inverted from listed mistakes): never store project memory in chat history; no coding before requirements are testable; no scope expansion without documentation; no completion claim without an evidence bundle; do not over-automate before the manual process stabilizes; avoid excessive process overhead

sources.evidence: WebFetch returned the page successfully. Title: "How to Build an AI Agent Harness: Step-by-Step Tutorial (2026)". Main headings: Understanding AI agent harness; Prerequisites to building an AI agent harness; How to build an AI agent harness in 2026; Where build sequences break; FAQ; How Atlan fits underneath any harness. Page contains an 11-item (Step 0 through Step 10) build sequence with Python/YAML/Markdown code examples and explicit "Done when" tests per step.
sources.summary: The page is a real Atlan knowledge-base tutorial on building an AI agent harness, framed around the thesis that "harness changes outperform model changes". It walks a Step 0-10 sequence: certify the data layer, scope the workflow and make a build-or-buy call, implement a minimal ReAct loop, write an AGENTS.md conventions file, layer a thin system prompt, enforce lineage-derived permissions, manage runtime context (glossary resolution), add an event-sourced persistence layer, instrument observability (tracing, cost, verification), add code-level guardrails with human checkpoints, and build evals from production traces. Each step ships with concrete code (Python loop, YAML permission/state configs, AGENTS.md template) and explicit "Done when" completion tests. The final section positions Atlan's metadata/lineage platform as the governance substrate underneath any harness, so it is vendor-flavored but the harness engineering content is substantive. Note: the claimed "10-step guide" is technically Step 0 through Step 10 (11 items), a trivial discrepancy.
patterns: Bounded ReAct loop: run_agent(task, tools, model, max_steps=10) iterating reason -> act -> observe, exiting on thought.is_final or raising StopCondition('max steps reached') at the step ceiling; 'push as much of the loop toward determinism as you can'

Per-step 'Done when' acceptance tests, e.g. Step 2: loop runs end-to-end, clean exits on success and step limit, all actions/observations logged; Step 10: evals derive from real traces, run on each release, regressions fail the build

AGENTS.md conventions file: short (fits on a screen or two), sections for Scope / Data it may read / Conventions / How to verify (e.g. 'run make eval-finance before declaring a task complete'); every rule must trace to a specific real failure; 'keep it short and point outward'
Thin system prompt as persona layer only, deferring rules to external docs (AGENTS.md); readable in under one minute; agent can name its single workflow and output format

Declarative permissions config (YAML): read gated to certified assets, write requires approval, destructive blocked by default; allow_list derived dynamically from lineage metadata (source: atlan_lineage, rule: upstream_of(certified_marts)) rather than a static file; 'enforce deterministically, not by politely asking the model'

Event-sourced persistence config (YAML): filesystem backend, append-only events, reload_on_boot: true so the agent resumes after crash, hands off to a fresh context window, and replays completed runs step-by-step for audit
Runtime context management: resolve glossary terms at query time per step, keep context length within a quality band rather than maximizing it
Observability trio: trace every run from the event log, expose per-task cost and latency, and run an automated verification check before the agent may declare a task complete

Guardrails as code, not prompts: 'a content filter that runs as code is a guarantee; a model instructed to behave is a hope'; deterministic input/output validation, human sign-off gate on irreversible actions, hard anti-infinite-loop controls

Evals as living regression tests: convert production failures into test cases, score with an independent scorer (never self-scoring, since 'a self-scoring agent inflates its own grade'), and wire eval regressions into CI to fail the build
Sequencing principle: certify/govern the data layer (Step 0) before any agent code, and scope the agent's job to one sentence before choosing build vs buy

sources.evidence: WebFetch returned the full article (no 404, no login wall). Page title: "Harness Engineering: How to Build Production-Ready LLM Agents That Actually Work" by Manoranjan Rajguru (monuminu), published May 21, 2026 on dev.to. Headings include "What Is a Harness? The Model Is Not the Agent", "Anatomy of the Agent Loop", "Guardrails: The Reliability Stack That Changes Everything", "Memory Across Sessions: CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, and Handoff Artifacts", "Tool Permissions and Security Enforcement", "Multi-Agent Orchestration with SlotWorker Patterns", and "Benchmarking Your Harness: Building a 26-Scenario Eval Suite". A second fetch extracted consistent code-level detail (Python dataclasses, retry/guardrail logic, eval metrics), confirming substantive matching content rather than a soft-404 or unrelated page.
sources.summary: The article argues that agent reliability comes from the harness, not the model: a small 8B model scored 53% baseline on a 26-scenario tool-use benchmark but 99% once wrapped in a proper harness ("Agent = Model + Harness"). It walks through the anatomy of a bounded agent loop (assemble context, stateless model call, validate, execute tools through the harness, iterate with a hard step cap), then covers the reliability stack: rescue parsing for malformed JSON, retry nudges for specific failure modes, and step enforcement to prevent workflow skipping. It treats the context window as the scarcest resource with tiered compaction and VRAM-aware token budgeting, covers cross-session memory via AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md files and JSON progress-file handoffs, enforces tool permissions in code (plus Docker sandboxing) rather than prompts, and describes a SlotWorker priority-queue pattern for sharing one GPU across multiple specialist agents. It closes with a tiered 26-scenario eval suite design for benchmarking harness changes.
patterns: Bounded ReAct-style agent loop: assemble layered context, stateless model call, guardrail validation, harness-mediated tool execution, append result, iterate with a hard max of 20 iterations

Rescue parsing as a 4-strategy cascade: direct JSON parse, extract first JSON object from surrounding text, clean common syntax issues (trailing commas, smart quotes), fallback to a lightweight model reformatting the malformed output
Retry nudges: classify the failure mode (invalid_json, wrong_tool, missing_required_field), append a targeted correction as a user-role message, cap at 3 retries before raising

Step enforcement: track required workflow steps in sequence, validate each proposed tool call against the next required step, block step-skipping and premature completion, return specific failure details

Tiered context compaction: keep last 4 tool-call/response pairs verbatim, compress older history with a small (~1.7B) model into messages prefixed [CONTEXT SUMMARY], reserve 1,024 tokens output headroom, hard-truncate as last resort
VRAM-aware token budgeting for local inference: ~0.35 MB/1K tokens (Q4_K_M), ~0.65 (Q8_0), ~1.30 (FP16); allocate 70% of free VRAM; fall back to 8,192 tokens if GPU detection fails

Cross-session memory via AGENTS.md injected into every session's system prompt (architecture, standards, prohibited actions, current work, definition of done) plus a .agent_progress.json handoff file with timestamps, completed steps, and artifacts for resumable sessions

PermissionConfig dataclass (allowed_read_dirs, allowed_write_dirs, network_allowed, require_approval_for) enforced in code, layered with a Docker sandbox (read-only rootfs, 100 MB writable /tmp, 512 MB memory, 50% CPU, no network, hard timeout); principle: enforcement code is law, prompts are suggestions

SlotWorker multi-agent orchestration: AgentJob dataclass with 1-10 priority, priority queue serializing access to a single GPU slot, concurrent submission with sequential execution (e.g. planner/security/performance specialists at priority 2, critic synthesizer at priority 1)

26-scenario tiered eval suite: Tier 1 single-tool calls, Tier 2 multi-step data-dependent chains, Tier 3 error recovery; BenchmarkScenario dataclass with required tool order, lambda success criteria, max steps (default 10); report pass rate overall and per tier (53% baseline vs 99% with harness)

sources.evidence: WebFetch returned the full page. Title: "Prompting Claude Fable 5". Headings include: Capability improvements; Longer turns by default; Consider all effort levels; Strong instruction following; Ground progress claims during long runs; State the boundaries; Parallel subagents; Construct a memory system; Rare cases of early stopping; Rare cases of context-budget concern; Give the reason, not only the request; Readability when communicating with the user; Create a send-to-user tool; Recommended scaffolding changes. The effort dial, fresh-context verifier subagents, and explicit boundary-instruction blocks are all present, matching the claim.
sources.summary: This is Anthropic's official platform docs page on prompting and scaffolding patterns for Claude Fable 5 (and Claude Mythos 5). It describes behavioral differences from Claude Opus 4.8: longer autonomous turns, stronger instruction following, more readiness to dispatch parallel subagents, and improved long-horizon autonomy. It positions the effort parameter (low/medium/high/xhigh) as the primary intelligence-latency-cost control and provides copy-paste prompt blocks for brevity, checkpoint behavior, grounding progress claims in tool evidence, boundary-setting (report findings vs. apply fixes), anti-overengineering, autonomous-pipeline continuation, and context-budget reassurance. It also covers building a simple file-based memory system, a client-side send_to_user tool schema with elicitation language, and scaffolding recommendations including fresh-context verifier subagents that outperform self-critique and auditing skills for show-your-reasoning instructions that trigger reasoning_extraction refusals.
patterns: Effort dial as primary harness control: default high, xhigh for capability-sensitive work, medium/low for routine; reduce effort rather than prompt-engineer when tasks complete but take too long

Fresh-context verifier subagents outperform self-critique: instruct 'Establish a method for checking your own work at an interval of [X]... verifying your work with subagents against the specification'
Boundary block: when the user is describing a problem, the deliverable is the assessment; report findings and stop; verify evidence supports a state-changing command before running it
Evidence-grounded progress reporting: 'Before reporting progress, audit each claim against a tool result from this session' — nearly eliminates fabricated status reports

Autonomous-pipeline anti-stall reminder: before ending turn, check last paragraph; if it is a plan/question/promise, do the work with tool calls now; end turn only when complete or blocked on user-only input
Checkpoint rule: pause only for destructive/irreversible actions, real scope changes, or input only the user can provide; ask and end turn rather than ending on a promise
Anti-overengineering block: no unrequested refactors/abstractions, validate only at system boundaries, no feature flags or backwards-compat shims when code can just change

send_to_user client-side tool (JSON schema with single required 'message' string) to surface verbatim content mid-turn; tool inputs are never summarized; must be paired with system-prompt elicitation language or the model rarely calls it

File-based memory system: one lesson per file with one-line summary at top; record corrections and confirmed approaches; update rather than duplicate; delete wrong notes; bootstrap by reflecting on past sessions with subagents

Parallel subagent guidance: delegate independent subtasks and keep working while they run; prefer async orchestrator-subagent communication over blocking; long-lived subagents save cost via cache reads
Context-budget handling: avoid showing remaining-token countdowns to the model; if shown, add reassurance 'You have ample context remaining. Do not stop, summarize, or suggest a new session'
Harness timeout redesign: individual requests can run many minutes and autonomous runs hours; adjust client timeouts and check runs asynchronously (scheduled jobs) rather than blocking
Migration guardrail: do not instruct the model to echo/transcribe internal reasoning in response text — triggers reasoning_extraction refusals; read structured thinking blocks instead
Skill/prompt refactoring: prompts written for prior models are often too prescriptive and can degrade output; a short behavioral instruction replaces enumerating each pattern
Intent framing template: 'I'm working on [larger task] for [who]. They need [what output enables]. With that in mind: [request]'

sources.evidence: WebFetch returned the full article (not a login wall or 404). Page title: "How to Actually Prompt Claude Fable 5", published June 11, 2026 by AlphaSignal. Headings observed: "Why more capability means fewer instructions", "The 4 deletions", "The 5 additions", "What the numbers say", "Where Fable 5 sits", "AlphaSignal Take", "Who benefits and who doesn't", "Practitioner Implication", "Appendix: the 5 prompt blocks worth copying". Two separate extraction prompts against the fetched page returned consistent details, including verbatim quotes and specific figures (~8% fallback rate to Opus 4.8, Stripe 50M-line Ruby migration example, GPT-5.5 comparison).
sources.summary: AlphaSignal's LinkedIn Pulse article distills Anthropic's official Claude Fable 5 prompting guidance into a practitioner playbook. Its core thesis is that prompt scaffolding built for prior models is too prescriptive for Fable 5 and actively degrades output quality. It prescribes four deletions (step-by-step instructions, demands for visible reasoning, token/context-budget countdowns, long enumerated behavior lists) and five additions (per-task effort settings, independently executable verification mechanisms/subagents, boundary blocks separating assessment from action, persistent memory files for cross-session learning, and stating the intent behind requests). It includes copy-paste prompt blocks in an appendix and candidly flags gaps: missing effect sizes, an unexplained ~8% task fallback rate to Opus 4.8, and undisclosed system interventions on 0.03% of requests. Supporting evidence cited includes Stripe's 50-million-line Ruby codebase migration and AlphaSignal's own ML-repo testing.
patterns: Delete prescriptive step-by-step task instructions for frontier models; over-specification degrades output quality
Remove demands for visible reasoning chains (e.g. 'think step by step'); the model plans and self-verifies on its own
Do not expose token or context-budget countdowns in prompts
Consolidate long enumerated behavior lists into brief high-level guidance
Add per-task effort settings (configurable effort level per invocation) to the harness
Provide verification mechanisms the model can execute independently, e.g. verification subagents or self-check loops before finalizing
Use boundary blocks in prompts that structurally separate assessment from action (guardrail against premature execution)
Maintain persistent memory files for cross-session learning rather than restating context each session
Pass the reasoning/intent behind a user request as context, not just the literal instruction
Harness-level model fallback routing: ~8% of Fable 5 tasks route to Opus 4.8, implying a fallback path should be designed for, not assumed away
Ship a small library of reusable prompt blocks (the article's appendix pattern) rather than bespoke prompts per task

Full body is paywalled; only the intro/framework overview was verifiable.
sources.summary: A paid Substack article by Linas Beliūnas on prompting Claude Fable 5 (and Mythos 5), framed around a deadline before Fable 5 usage moves off the free plan on June 22, 2026. It argues that prompting habits from earlier Claude models degrade Fable 5 output and promises 12 core prompting patterns plus practical workflows (codebase audits, market research). The free preview confirms the claimed AI-OS framework by exact name: a four-layer Context-Connections-Capabilities-Cadence system. It also previews an orchestrator-with-cheaper-workers routing architecture, a "set goals, not steps" mental model, a loop architecture for autonomous operation, a markdown memory system, and screenshot-first vision workflows. The 12 patterns themselves are behind the paywall, so only the framework outline could be verified, but the page substantially matches the charter's citation.
patterns: Four-layer AI-OS framework: Context, Connections, Capabilities, Cadence — a layered decomposition for structuring an agent operating environment
Orchestrator-and-cheaper-workers routing: a strong model plans/routes while cheaper models execute subtasks
Set goals, not steps: prompt the agent with outcomes and constraints rather than procedural instructions
Loop architecture: run the agent in a persistent loop so it acts as an autonomous operating partner rather than one-shot Q&A
Markdown memory system: durable plain-text markdown files as the agent's memory substrate
Screenshot-first vision workflows: feed screenshots as the primary input channel for UI/visual tasks
Caveat: the 12 concrete prompting patterns are paywalled; only the framework skeleton above was verifiable from the free preview

Section headings (with ids): Prompt for Autonomy Not Dialogue (mindset), Calibrating the Effort Setting (effort), Prompting for Self-Verification (self-verify), Context Engineering for Long Sessions (context), Prompt Caching and Cost Control (caching), Prompting Around the Safeguards (safeguards), plus FAQ and a Sources section citing Anthropic's June 9, 2026 announcement and Artificial Analysis benchmarks.
sources.summary: The page is a real 11-minute blog post by the Lushbinary Team (an AI/cloud consultancy in Newark, DE) titled "Claude Fable 5 Prompting Guide: Effort & Self-Check", published June 11, 2026, matching the charter's citation. It argues Fable 5 (Anthropic's first Mythos-class model, $10/$50 per million tokens, slow time-to-first-token) rewards autonomous goal-oriented prompting rather than chat-style turn-by-turn interaction. It covers calibrating the effort setting as a per-task dial (highest effort adds a reflect-and-validate pass before returning), prompting patterns that amplify self-verification, context engineering for long sessions (external memory, summarize finished work, retrieve-not-recall), prompt caching for the 90% cached-input discount, and handling the safety-classifier fallback that hands responses to Opus 4.8. A July 1, 2026 update note covers Fable 5 access being restored after a June 12 export-control suspension. Sources cited are Anthropic's June 9, 2026 announcement and Artificial Analysis benchmarks, with a note that prompting recommendations are Lushbinary's own.
patterns: Prompt for autonomy: state the goal and machine-checkable success criteria, not scripted micro-steps; give the model tools (tests, file access, search) so reliability comes from self-verification, and batch all context up front into fewer, richer turns because time-to-first-token is high

Effort as a per-task dial, not a global setting: highest effort (adds self-reflect-and-validate pass) for open-ended high-stakes work, medium for well-defined reasoning, low for routine steps that likely belong on a cheaper model; instrument cost and latency at each level per workload

Self-verification amplifiers: always provide a concrete verification target (tests, schema, reference output, acceptance criteria); ask the model to list ways its answer could be wrong and address them before finalizing; separate generation from review by running a second reviewer agent on the diff plus the requirement, not on the reasoning trace that produced it

Deterministic gates over self-checking: run test suite, type checker, and build regardless of model self-verification; require human approval before irreversible actions; reversibility should gate autonomy

Context engineering for long sessions: persist plan, decisions, and findings to an external memory store the model reads from instead of carrying the transcript (file-based memory reportedly improved Fable 5 3x more than Opus 4.8); compress completed workstreams into short summaries; retrieve only the slice the current step needs

Cost structure for harness prompts: keep system prompt and stable project context as a byte-identical cached prefix at the front (90% cached-input discount), place volatile per-step content last so it does not invalidate the cache, and right-size output since output tokens cost 5x input

Safeguard-fallback handling: detect and log the API signal that a response was handed off to the fallback model (Opus 4.8) so you know when you paid Fable 5 rates for a fallback answer; make benign intent explicit for security/bio/chem work; route workloads that reliably trip classifiers directly to the fallback model

sources.summary: The page is a live Conversion System blog article on how to prompt Anthropic's Claude Fable 5 model, matching the citation. It presents a 7-step prompting recipe: open with the desired outcome, give the reason/audience, set boundaries, pick an effort level, demand evidence-backed progress claims, request an outcome-first readable summary, and schedule self-verification on long jobs. It maps the four effort levels (low for lookups/reformatting, medium for everyday documents, high as the recommended default for real projects, xhigh for the hardest problems) and defines a pause policy: ask the user only for irreversible actions, plan changes, or information only the user has. It includes three copy-paste prompt templates (everyday task, document/analysis, long-running project), an 8-line cheat sheet, and six common mistakes such as porting long rule lists from earlier models and giving vague success criteria.
patterns: Prompt structure: outcome-first framing (state the finished result, then context/audience/purpose, then boundaries) instead of step-by-step procedural instructions
Explicit effort-level parameter (low/medium/high/xhigh) selected per task class: low=lookups/reformatting, medium=everyday docs, high=default for real projects, xhigh=hardest consequential problems

Interrupt/escalation guardrail: agent pauses to ask the human only when the action is irreversible, deviates from the agreed plan, or requires information only the human possesses; otherwise runs to completion
Evidence-gated progress reporting: every progress claim on long runs must be tied to verifiable work performed ('require receipts'), reporting only verified progress
Scheduled self-verification loop for long jobs: periodically re-check work against the original goal/definition-of-done and record lesson notes
Persistent lessons/notes file maintained between sessions as lightweight cross-session memory

Long-running task prompt template: 'Goal: [outcome + definition of done]. Work end-to-end without waiting. Pause only for irreversible changes, plan shifts, or info only I have. Verify work against goal regularly. Report only verified progress.'

Anti-pattern guardrails: strip long inherited rule lists from older-model prompts, distinguish assessment from action requests, make success criteria checkable by third parties, do not add unrequested features

sources.evidence: WebFetch succeeded (two fetches). Page title: "How to prompt Claude Fable 5: what changes and why". Headings: What actually changed; Effort is your main dial; Say less, steer with intent; Keep long runs honest; State the boundaries; The migration gotcha: don't ask it to show its reasoning; Let it delegate, and give it a memory. Caveat: the article covers subagent delegation ("Fable 5 dispatches parallel subagents more readily and manages them reliably") but does NOT explicitly describe an orchestrator-and-cheaper-workers cost-routing pattern; the only cheaper-model routing mentioned is a safety-classifier fallback to Claude Opus 4.8.
sources.summary: The page is a real prompting guide for Claude Fable 5 hosted on kbcafe.com. It describes three behavioral shifts in the model (longer runs, stronger instruction-following, more autonomous initiative) and recommends using the effort parameter (low/medium/high/xhigh) as the primary control dial rather than verbose prompting. It advises intent-and-boundaries prompting over enumerated checklists, verification of progress claims against session tool results, explicit boundaries around human decision points, and file-based memory for lessons learned. It warns that asking the model to show its reasoning triggers safety classifiers and fallback routing to Claude Opus 4.8. The citation's "orchestrator-and-cheaper-workers routing" framing overstates the delegation section, which endorses parallel subagent dispatch but never prescribes routing to cheaper worker models.
patterns: Effort parameter as the primary control dial: high (default), xhigh for capability-sensitive work, medium/low for routine tasks — tune effort instead of padding prompts
Intent-and-boundaries prompting: short directives stating goal and limits outperform exhaustive enumerated checklists for strong instruction-followers
Claim-audit loop for long runs: before reporting progress, audit each claim against a tool result from the current session (anti-hallucinated-progress guardrail)
Assessment-vs-action guardrail: when the user is describing a problem, the deliverable is the assessment — report findings and stop rather than autonomously fixing
File-based agent memory: one lesson per file with a one-line summary at the top; record both corrections and confirmed approaches
Parallel subagent delegation: Fable 5 dispatches and manages parallel subagents reliably, so harnesses can lean on fan-out delegation (note: article does not specify cheaper worker models)

Migration gotcha as harness rule: never prompt the model to reveal its reasoning — it triggers safety classifiers and silent fallback routing to a different model (Claude Opus 4.8), changing behavior mid-run

sources.evidence: WebFetch resolved the URL directly (no redirect, no error page). Page title: "Claude Fable 5 — Technical Harness Report", authored by Svngoku (NIONGOLO Chrys Fé-Marty), published July 1, 2026 on the Hugging Face community blog. Nine headings observed including "The safety-classifier pipeline", "Agentic harness topologies", "Messages API contract deltas", and "Quick-reference parameter cheat sheet" — matching the claimed topic (technical harness report with safety pipeline coverage).
sources.summary: The article is a developer-facing technical report on Anthropic's Claude Fable 5 model, focused on what harness builders need to change. It covers the model family and capability tiering (Fable 5 shares weights with the unsafeguarded Mythos 5 variant, differing by a runtime safety-classifier layer), the always-on adaptive thinking system with a five-level effort parameter replacing the old thinking on/off + budget_tokens model, and a two-stage safety pipeline (cheap activation probe, then LLM classifier) covering cybersecurity, bio/chem, distillation, and frontier-LLM-development risk domains with per-surface fallback behavior. It documents Messages API contract deltas: a new tokenizer (~30% more tokens), no assistant prefill (HTTP 400), a new "refusal" stop reason, 1M context, and 128k max output. It closes with three multi-agent orchestration topologies (blocking orchestrator, fixed-agent team, async subagents) with benchmark tradeoffs, plus practical guardrail, memory, and compaction guidance.
patterns: Three orchestration topologies with explicit tradeoffs: blocking orchestrator (simplest, slowest, context re-established per subtask), fixed-agent team of 3-10 peers with Send Message/Wait for Message primitives (highest scores, 93.3% BrowseComp, highest token cost), async subagents returning results via messages (best latency/token tradeoff, ~1.6x speedup concentrated in the hard tail of tasks)

Effort parameter pattern: sweep effort per task class (low/medium/high/xhigh/max) instead of hardcoding max; cost plateaus before maximum; thinking cannot be disabled (thinking type disabled returns HTTP 400); thinking.display is 'omitted' or 'summarized' and raw chain-of-thought is never returned, so never parse thinking output for structured signal

Refusal-handling loop: always check stop_reason before reading content; stop_reason 'refusal' returns a structured category; pre-output refusals are unbilled while mid-stream refusals bill streamed tokens; opt-in beta 'fallbacks' parameter (e.g. fallbacks: [{type: 'model', model: 'claude-opus-4-8'}]) enables server-side retry instead of client retry logic

Two-stage safety pipeline shape: cheap activation probe on all traffic, escalation to an LLM classifier for flagged requests, with per-deployment-surface fallback contracts (consumer apps auto-fallback with notification; raw API blocks by default)
Guardrails for destructive actions: enforce allow-lists and dry-run gates on filesystem/DB-mutating tools because sandbox destructive-action risk is higher than the prior generation

Config/beta-header patterns: task-budgets-2026-03-13 header replaces manual token-ceiling logic; context-management-2025-06-27 enables tool-result clearing for long-horizon runs; compaction is opt-in with a configurable threshold (e.g. 200k) and should be A/B tested per task distribution

Token budgeting: new tokenizer emits ~30% more tokens than Opus-tier for the same text, so use the count_tokens endpoint rather than heuristics; no assistant prefill — use output_config.format structured outputs or system prompt instead
Memory architecture: wire a file-based persistent memory tool for multi-session deployments (reported ~3x larger gains for this model class than the prior tier)
Prompting/reliability: prefer outcome-focused instructions over over-specified step-by-step prompts; design orchestration for single requests running minutes (async/polling, not synchronous waits)

Perplexity sonar-pro (HTTP 200) independently confirmed existence and summarized content, citing the video itself, Nate Herk's LinkedIn post about it, geeky-gadgets.com/anthropic-engineers-prompt-fable-5/, and multiple Skool community posts announcing the video.
sources.summary: The video is real: a short practical piece by the Nate Herk | AI Automation channel presenting six prompting habits for Fable 5, framed as what Anthropic engineers actually do. Techniques covered: give the model the "why" (task intent/context), use explicit negative instructions, let the model act once it has enough information instead of forcing exhaustive planning, and require evidence of completion rather than accepting a bare "done". It also warns that asking Fable 5 to show its reasoning can trigger refusal or safety-routing behavior (routing high-risk requests to a different model), and advises matching instruction size and effort level (high/xhigh for heavy work, medium for interactive) to the task. Caveat: the content summary rests on transcript excerpts and companion posts surfaced by Perplexity, not a full verified transcript, though the title/channel match is confirmed directly via YouTube's oembed API.
patterns: Context grounding: include the task's intent (the 'why') in agent prompts so the model connects the request to the right information instead of guessing
Negative prompting: encode explicit do-not constraints (style, scope, irrelevant detail) alongside positive instructions
Anti-overplanning guardrail: instruct the agent to begin acting once it has sufficient information rather than requiring a complete upfront plan
Completion verification: require the agent to prove it is done with evidence or a pointer to the result (verify-before-report), never accept a bare 'done'
Avoid requesting explicit chain-of-thought from Fable 5: it can trigger refusals or safety routing; let the harness rely on the model's native reasoning instead
Effort-level matching as a config knob: high/xhigh reasoning effort for heavy autonomous work, medium for fast interactive loops
Instruction-size budgeting: keep prompts proportional to task complexity; oversized instruction blocks waste tokens and degrade adherence
Account for provider-side safety routing: high-risk-looking requests may be silently routed to a different model, so harnesses should detect and handle degraded responses

Updated July 2026." Section headings: Why Fable 5 Is Strong for Building Apps; Where It Fits, and Where It Does Not; A Build Harness With Verification Gates; Front End and UI Generation Strengths; Backend, API, and Data Layer Generation; Routing Routine Work to Opus 4.8; Cost Math for a Real Project; FAQ. sources.summary: The page is a real, substantial (13-min-read class) LushBinary blog guide on building full-stack apps with Claude Fable 5, updated July 2026. Its core is a build-harness pattern: the model plans and builds, deterministic tools run checks, verification gates decide acceptance, and a human approves the merge. It describes a five-stage loop (Plan, Build, Verify, Gate, Review) where the plan is a human-reviewed contract, gates are the test suite, type checker, linter, and build, red gates feed failure output back for bounded retries before human escalation, and merges to main are always human-owned. It also covers where Fable 5 fits (greenfield, well-tested codebases, prototypes) versus where it does not (low-test-coverage legacy), routing routine work to the cheaper Opus 4.8 at half the price, and per-task cost math ($4.50/task example, ~150-task builds, ~$675 all-Fable, prompt caching cutting cached input cost ~90%).
patterns: Five-stage harness loop: Plan -> Build -> Verify -> Gate -> Review, where the plan (task list: data model, API endpoints, UI screens, tests-that-define-done) is a human-reviewed contract before any code is generated

Deterministic, non-negotiable verification gates run on every change: test suite, type checker, linter, and build; model self-verification (high effort mode) is a default assist, never a substitute for these checks
Gate semantics: on green, auto-commit to a branch; on red, feed the failure output back to the model for a bounded number of retries, then escalate to a human
Human-owned merge: the agent prepares and proves work on a branch; a person reviews the diff and owns the decision to merge to main
Small reversible commits so a bad batch is a rollback, not a forensic exercise

Tests-as-enabler rule: on greenfield builds have the model write tests alongside code as the first phase; on untested codebases backfill critical-path coverage before any unattended runs; if correctness cannot be expressed as pass/fail checks, do not run unattended

Difficulty-based model routing: premium model (Fable 5, $10/$50 per Mtok) only for planning, hard UI/interaction logic, ambiguous integration, and debugging gate failures; cheaper model (Opus 4.8, $5/$25) for boilerplate/CRUD, config wiring, repetitive tests, and docs

Cost instrumentation: meter tokens per task from day one, set a hard budget cap on the harness, estimate project cost as task-count x per-task cost (example: 200k in + 50k out = $4.50/task on Fable 5), and use prompt caching of a shared context prefix for ~90% input discount
Async batch operation: the loop runs in the background (model is slow to first token, built for long-horizon async work) while the human reviews completed batches

sources.evidence: WebFetch returned HTTP 403 (bot blocking), but curl with a browser User-Agent returned HTTP 200 with a 168KB page. Title: "Claude Code Dynamic Workflows: Harness Guide | Lushbinary". H1: "A Harness for Every Task: Claude Code Dynamic Workflows Explained", dated June 3, 2026, 13 min read. Numbered section headings include "1 What a Harness Actually Is", "2 Why a Single Context Window Breaks Down", "4 The Six Orchestration Patterns" (classify-and-act, fan-out-and-synthesize, adversarial verification, generate-and-filter, tournament, loop-until-done), plus FAQ and a Sources section citing Anthropic's June 2, 2026 "A harness for every task" post and the Claude Code docs.
sources.summary: The page is a real, substantive blog guide by consultancy Lushbinary (published June 3, 2026) explaining Anthropic's "A harness for every task" announcement of dynamic workflows in Claude Code, shipped alongside Claude Opus 4.8. It defines a harness as the system around the model (work splitting, subagent spawning, tool scoping, verification, model routing, isolation, stop conditions) and explains that Claude now writes a task-specific JavaScript orchestration harness executed by a background runtime. It names three failure modes of single-context runs (agentic laziness, self-preferential bias, goal drift) and presents exactly the six orchestration patterns the claim references: classify-and-act, fan-out-and-synthesize, adversarial verification, generate-and-filter, tournament, and loop-until-done. It also covers non-coding use cases (deep research, fact-checking, ticket triage, resume ranking, session mining), triggering via the "ultracode" keyword or /effort ultracode, saving scripts to .claude/workflows/ or ~/.claude/workflows/, runtime limits (16 concurrent agents, 1,000 per run), and when workflows are not worth the tokens. The article is marketing-wrapped (Lushbinary services, WidelAI affiliate promo) but the technical content is detailed and sourced to Anthropic publications.
patterns: Six composable orchestration patterns: classify-and-act (classifier agent routes items to per-type handlers), fan-out-and-synthesize (many isolated-context agents plus a synthesize barrier that waits for all), adversarial verification (a separate challenger agent checks each producer agent's output against a rubric), generate-and-filter (overgenerate candidates, rubric-filter, dedupe near-duplicates), tournament (N agents attempt the same task differently, judge agents compare pairwise because comparative judgment beats absolute scoring), loop-until-done (keep spawning agents until a stop condition like no-new-findings, not a fixed pass count)

Three structural failure modes a harness must counter: agentic laziness (declaring done at partial progress), self-preferential bias (model grades its own output generously, so verification must be a different agent than the producer), goal drift (objective fidelity decays across turns and lossy compactions, so give each subagent one focused goal in a fresh context)

Harness-as-artifact: the orchestration layer is a plain JavaScript script with spawn/coordinate primitives plus standard JSON/Math/Array processing, executed by a separate background runtime; the script chooses per-step model tier and per-subagent worktree isolation, and is readable, rerunnable, editable, and shareable
Context hygiene guardrail: intermediate subagent tool traces never enter the main session context; only the final converged result is ingested

Runaway-loop guardrails: hard caps of 16 concurrent agents (fewer on low-core machines) and 1,000 agents total per run; cost control by prompting an explicit token budget (e.g. 'use 10k tokens') and starting on a narrow scope before widening
Resumability: interrupted runs resume within the same session with completed agents returning cached results (memoized subagent outputs)

Permission model: spawned subagents always run in acceptEdits mode and inherit the parent tool allowlist; off-allowlist shell/web/MCP calls pause the run for approval, so pre-populate the allowlist before long unattended runs

Quarantine pattern for prompt-injection defense: agents that read untrusted content (tickets, scraped pages, email) are barred from high-privilege actions; a separate set of agents acts on the extracted information

Persistence and distribution: save a successful workflow script as a slash command in .claude/workflows/ (repo-shared) or ~/.claude/workflows/ (user-global), parameterized via an args global; distribute by embedding the JS in a skill referenced from SKILL.md, treated as a template rather than run verbatim

Triggering conventions: plain-language opt-in ('use a workflow'), the 'ultracode' keyword to force one task into a workflow (formerly the literal keyword 'workflow' before v2.1.160), or /effort ultracode to plan a workflow for every substantive task; compose with /loop for scheduling and /goal for hard completion requirements

When-not-to-use litmus: workflows cost significantly more tokens, so skip them when the change is small, one context window suffices, a single pass is trustworthy, or a handful of edits gets it done — ask whether the task really needs more compute

sources.summary: The video is real: Jordan Urbs, published 2026-06-29, demonstrating that GLM 5.2 (an open-weight model) run inside an agentic harness in the OpenCode desktop app can produce Claude-level ("Fable 5") results without a subscription. The demo task is a full SEO/AEO audit of a website, completed for roughly $9 in API costs. The harness is framed as an audit workflow/template (checklist-driven prompt-skill) rather than a freeform agent, and it uses a team of role-specialized subagents that proof each other's work before results are returned. Chapter markers show a design-reviewer agent catching the primary model's error (a wrong color) during the review phase. The exact 11-agent/6-phase count in the charter could not be confirmed from indexed metadata, but the overall multi-agent phased audit-harness description substantially matches the video's actual content.
patterns: Model-swap harness: run a cheap open-weight model (GLM 5.2) inside a Claude Code-style agentic harness (OpenCode) so the harness structure, not the frontier model, carries the quality; whole AEO audit cost ~$9
Audit-as-workflow framing: the harness is an audit template/checklist (a reusable prompt-skill), not a freeform agent session; phases follow the checklist
Role-specialized subagent team: multiple named reviewer roles (e.g. design-reviewer) rather than one generalist agent
Cross-review guardrail: agents proof each other's work; a dedicated reviewer caught the primary model's error (wrong color) before handoff
Final validation gate: the harness reviews everything to make sure it's good before giving results back to the user (generate -> review -> return loop)

Headings: Why Archon?, What It Looks Like, What Can You Automate?, Architecture, Web UI, Getting Started. Stats: 22.7k stars, 3.4k forks, latest release v0.5.0 (June 26, 2026), TypeScript 96.8%, MIT license.
sources.summary: Archon (coleam00/Archon) is an open-source workflow engine and self-described harness builder for AI coding, aiming to make AI-assisted coding deterministic and repeatable. Developers define development processes as YAML workflows in .archon/workflows/ that orchestrate planning, implementation, validation, code review, and PR creation phases. Each workflow run is isolated in its own git worktree so runs execute in parallel without conflicts, and workflows mix deterministic nodes (bash scripts, tests, git operations) with AI-powered nodes for planning and code generation. It offers both a CLI and a web dashboard for monitoring runs and managing projects, with triggers/integrations across Slack, Telegram, Discord, and GitHub. It ships with 19 pre-built workflows (e.g., fix GitHub issue, create PR, architectural review) and supports custom YAML-authored workflows. The claimed description (harness/agent builder for coding workflows, YAML workflows, CLI/Web orchestration) substantially matches the actual repo content.
patterns: Workflows-as-config: development processes declared as YAML files in a repo-local .archon/workflows/ directory, versioned alongside the code they operate on
Phase-structured workflow loop: planning -> implementation -> validation -> code review -> PR creation as explicit orchestrated stages rather than one open-ended agent loop
Hybrid node types: deterministic nodes (bash scripts, test runs, git operations) interleaved with AI nodes (planning, code generation), keeping verifiable steps out of the LLM's hands
Isolation guardrail: every workflow run executes in its own git worktree, enabling parallel runs with no cross-contamination of working state
Prebuilt workflow library (19 templates, e.g. fix-GitHub-issue, create-PR, architectural review) as a starting vocabulary users extend with custom YAML
Dual control surface: CLI for invocation plus a web dashboard for run monitoring, project management, and activity review
Multi-channel triggering/integration: workflows reachable from Slack, Telegram, Discord, and GitHub, decoupling harness invocation from the developer's terminal

Corroborated by https://github.com/coleam00/Archon README, which uses the identical "first open-source harness builder for AI coding" framing. Perplexity API was unreachable from this network (POSTs dropped, HTTP 000/exit 52) but was not needed given direct YouTube confirmation.
sources.summary: The video is real and is exactly what the charter claims: Cole Medin's April 2026 announcement/tutorial for Archon, an open-source harness builder for AI coding. He frames the progression from prompt engineering to harness engineering, defining a harness as "the system around the coding agent" that automates repetitive multi-step workflows. The video covers encoding workflows as YAML files stored in the repo, executing them via CLI, web UI, Slack, and GitHub, and combining deterministic steps with AI-driven steps and iterative testing loops. It includes a live demo of building a custom workflow, plus chapters on installation, end-to-end workflows, dashboard features, and parallel execution. The companion GitHub repo (github.com/coleam00/Archon) documents the same system: a layered architecture from platform adapters through an orchestrator to AI assistant clients and a SQLite/PostgreSQL persistence layer.
patterns: Workflows encoded as repo-committed YAML files, making harness behavior versioned, deterministic, and repeatable rather than ad hoc prompting
Hybrid node composition: deterministic nodes (bash scripts, tests, git operations) interleaved with AI nodes, with the principle that AI only runs where it adds value
Isolated git worktrees per workflow run to enable conflict-free parallel execution of multiple agent tasks

Layered harness architecture: platform adapters (Web UI, CLI, Telegram, Slack, Discord, GitHub) -> orchestrator handling message routing and context -> command handlers / workflow executors -> pluggable AI assistant clients (Claude, Codex, Pi) -> database layer (SQLite/PostgreSQL)
Persistent state store for codebases, conversations, sessions, and workflow runs, giving durability and auditability across runs
Standard workflow stage decomposition: plan -> implement -> validate -> code review -> PR creation
Iterative testing loop pattern: AI iterates against deterministic validation steps until checks pass
Multi-surface triggering: the same YAML workflow is invocable from CLI, web UI, Slack, or GitHub events
Model-agnostic assistant client abstraction so the harness is not bound to a single LLM vendor

findings.claim: Anthropic publishes a dedicated official page 'Prompting Claude Fable 5' covering behavioral differences from Claude Opus 4.8 and prompt/scaffolding changes: effort levels, instruction following, long-run progress claims, memory systems, parallel subagents, and the reasoning_extraction refusal category. This is the canonical source for Fable 5 prompting guidance.

OFFICIAL effort guidance: 'Effort is the primary control for the trade-off between intelligence, latency, and cost on Claude Fable 5. Use high as the default for most tasks, with xhigh for the most capability-sensitive workloads and medium or low for routine work. Lower effort settings on Claude Fable 5 still perform well and often exceed xhigh performance on prior models.' API mechanics (corroborated by the bundled claude-api skill, cached 2026-06-24): set output_config.effort to low|medium|high|xhigh|max (GA, no beta header); on Fable 5 thinking is always on and adaptive-only, so omit the thinking parameter entirely; thinking:{type:'disabled'} and budget_tokens both return 400. Run effort sweeps including low/medium; reduce effort if tasks complete correctly but slowly.

OFFICIAL verifier-subagent guidance (Recommended scaffolding changes section): 'Make self-verification explicit in long-run prompts. Separate, fresh-context verifier subagents tend to outperform self-critique. For long-running tasks, instruct: Establish a method for checking your own work at an interval of [X] as you build. Run this every [X interval], verifying your work with subagents against the specification.' Related: Fable 5 dispatches parallel subagents more readily than prior models; Anthropic recommends using subagents frequently with explicit delegation guidance and asynchronous (non-blocking) orchestrator-subagent communication, since long-lived subagents keep context and save cost via cache reads.

OFFICIAL instruction-following guidance vs prior models: instruction following is improved enough that a brief instruction steers most behaviors, so do not enumerate each behavior. Key directives: (a) de-prescribe: 'Skills developed for prior models are often too prescriptive for Claude Fable 5 and can degrade output quality', prefer stating goal and constraints over step-by-step scaffolding, A/B test with old scaffolding removed; (b) ground progress claims against tool results ('nearly eliminated fabricated status reports' in Anthropic testing); (c) state explicit boundaries to prevent unrequested adjacent actions; (d) add anti-overplanning language ('When you have enough information to act, act'); (e) never instruct the model to echo or transcribe its internal reasoning in the response, which triggers the reasoning_extraction refusal; read summarized thinking blocks instead; (f) for autonomous pipelines, add an anti-early-stopping system reminder; (g) pair a client-side send_to_user tool with explicit elicitation language for verbatim mid-task delivery.

The cross-model 'Prompting best practices' page loads and is the living reference for all current models including Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5. Fable-relevant confirmations: on Fable 5/Mythos 5 thinking is always on (adaptive is the only mode); budget_tokens returns 400 on Opus 4.7+ and Fable/Mythos 5; last-turn assistant prefills return 400 starting with Claude 4.6 models; effort plus query complexity calibrate thinking depth. Instruction-following delta vs the Claude 4 era: models from Opus 4.5/4.6 onward follow the system prompt much more closely, so dial back aggressive language ('CRITICAL: You MUST use this tool when...' becomes 'Use this tool when...') and remove 'If in doubt, use [tool]' style anti-laziness prompting, which now overtriggers.

The prompt engineering overview URL loads on platform.claude.com. It is a short hub page (success criteria first, prompt generator, tutorials) that routes model-specific tuning to the 'Prompting best practices' page; it contains no Fable-specific content itself.

The docs.claude.com variant of the overview URL responds with a 302 redirect to the identical path on platform.claude.com (the canonical docs host). The URL is live but serves no content directly; content was verified at the redirect target.

The official announcement 'Introducing Claude Fable 5 and Claude Mythos 5' loads (fetched via the www form given in the task; normalized here per URL conventions). It announces two Mythos-class models released June 9, 2026: Claude Fable 5 (GA with safety safeguards) and Claude Mythos 5 (same model, safeguards lifted for authorized users). Both were briefly suspended June 12 and redeployed July 1, 2026. Pricing $10 per million input tokens and $50 per million output tokens. The announcement contains NO prompting guidance (no effort parameter, no verifier subagents); that lives only in the platform docs.

Perplexity sonar-pro (queried successfully via streaming after repeated non-streaming connection drops) independently confirms: Anthropic's only official prompt-engineering guidance for the Claude 5 family is the platform docs Fable 5 prompting guide plus the system card; there is no separate official effort-parameter doc or verifier cookbook beyond the docs. It explicitly flags YouTube videos, LinkedIn posts, Substack guides (linas.substack.com, lushbinary.com, productcompass.pm), and an alleged 'Fable 5 system prompt leak' blog post as community interpretation, not Anthropic policy.

Perplexity's citations also surfaced an Anthropic news post on redeploying Fable 5 (corroborating the June 12 suspension and July 1 redeploy mentioned in the announcement) and a system card PDF on www-cdn.anthropic.com. I did not fetch these two URLs directly, so they are unverified pointers, though both are on official Anthropic domains.

ground_truth.summary:
Anthropic's real, current, official prompting guidance for Claude Fable 5 (Claude 5 family, released June 9, 2026) exists and lives on platform.claude.com. Method: WebFetch of all three candidate URLs plus the two docs pages they route to, cross-checked against the bundled claude-api reference skill (cached 2026-06-24) and a Perplexity sonar-pro query (succeeded only via streaming; non-streaming requests were dropped mid-flight).

URL status: platform.claude.com prompt-engineering/overview loads (hub page, routes to 'Prompting best practices'); docs.claude.com variant 302-redirects to the platform.claude.com equivalent; the anthropic.com news announcement loads and confirms Fable 5 (safeguarded GA) and Mythos 5 (safeguards lifted, authorized users), $10/$50 per MTok, June 9 launch, June 12 suspension, July 1 redeploy, but contains zero prompting guidance. The canonical prompting source is the dedicated page /docs/en/build-with-claude/prompt-engineering/prompting-claude-fable-5, which I fetched in full.

Confirmed official guidance, three areas requested: (1) Effort: output_config.effort (low|medium|high|xhigh|max, GA) is the primary intelligence/latency/cost control; default high, xhigh for capability-sensitive work, medium/low for routine work, and low on Fable 5 often beats xhigh on prior models. Thinking is always on and adaptive-only: omit the thinking parameter; disabled and budget_tokens both 400. (2) Verifier subagents: official docs state 'Separate, fresh-context verifier subagents tend to outperform self-critique' and give a verbatim instruction template ('Establish a method for checking your own work... verifying your work with subagents against the specification'); use parallel subagents freely with asynchronous, non-blocking orchestration. (3) Instruction following vs Claude 4: markedly stronger and more literal; brief instructions replace enumerated behavior lists; prior-model prompts and skills are 'often too prescriptive' and degrade quality, so de-prescribe and A/B with scaffolding removed; dial back 'CRITICAL/MUST' tool language (overtriggers from 4.5/4.6 onward); prefills 400 on 4.6+; add grounded-progress-claims, explicit-boundaries, anti-overplanning, and anti-early-stopping snippets (all quoted verbatim in the docs); never ask the model to echo its reasoning (triggers reasoning_extraction refusal, read summarized thinking blocks instead).

Community speculation, clearly distinguished: Substack guides, YouTube walkthroughs, LinkedIn threads, and an alleged 'system prompt leak' post circulate but are not Anthropic guidance; Perplexity and my direct fetches agree the platform docs pages above are the only official prompting sources, alongside the announcement, a redeployment news post, and the system card (the latter two on official domains but not fetched directly).
